title: Bitcoin: A Bear Market State of Mind
author: IntoTheCryptoverse
summary_bullets: Hey everyone and thanks for jumping back into the Cryptoverse.
Today, we're going to talk about Bitcoin, a bear market state of mind.
Let's go ahead and jump in.
So yesterday we put out a video talking about what Bitcoin normally does in the first week of March.
And I had some people reach out and ask, why does it have to yield a lower high?
First of all, it doesn't have to do anything.
I could absolutely be wrong about the market.

But for me, ever since Q4 of last year, I've been operating under a bear market state of mind where I just naturally assume that, especially early in a midterm year, that lower highs will in fact be lower highs and they will not yield higher highs, and that lows will eventually lead to lower lows.
So I want to talk about that and kind of go through some of the prior midterm years.
In the first midterm year we had, after like a major rally, you can see that Bitcoin had several lower lows throughout the entire year.

extracted_rules: It can be difficult, of course, to pivot to being in a bear market state of mind even when you're just used to watching Bitcoin go up three years in a row.
But if you're going to pivot to that bear market state of mind, the best time to do so usually is in the fourth quarter of the post-having year.
If we go look at all the days for each year, we could sort of put those in like a table like we did yesterday.
If it is, that's the most pathetic bear market move in March we've seen.
And one of the reasons is when you look at the prior bear market rallies in March that have occurred, they got us back up to the bear market resistance band, right?
Maybe 85K when we look at weekly candles.

And what I would say is you have to at least look at this pattern right here as well, where after Bitcoin broke down from the highs, after I broke down from the highs last cycle, if you exclude seasonality, you can see that Bitcoin really struggled for a few weeks and then eventually it then dumped below the 200-week moving average, right?
Seems like that could be when the next major low for Bitcoin forms.
And if you look at the year to date ROI, let me actually reload this because it probably hasn't updated with today's data.

If you look at 2026 and look at the average of prior midterm years and then we look at one standard deviation, Bitcoin is currently underperforming the average of prior midterm years, not by a lot, but by a little bit.
If it were actually at the average, Bitcoin would only be down about 15% from the yearly open.
Well, the yearly open, if we go back up to where Bitcoin was, say like in January, 15% down would put Bitcoin in say like the mid 70s.

And so when I look at a chart like this, it's hard to know when each move is going to happen, when we actually get those moves back up to the top of the bands, when we go to the lower part of the bands.

So if you look at this though, if you look at the year to date ROI, what you'll notice is that on average, the next drop tends to start, doesn't mean the high starts yet then, but the next drop can often really get underway once we get into late March going into April.
So late March going into April is when on average Bitcoin really starts to drop.

Now every single year is different, but you can see how Bitcoin does tend to hold up for a little while, but then as you get out into late March, April, May timeframe, that's usually when the floor falls out and that's usually when the market goes lower.
If we look at 2026 and compare it to say 2014, you'll see that Bitcoin kind of the same thing, right?
I don't want to tell you incorrectly if that's wrong, but I think that's how it played out.
Yeah, technically it was April 2nd or April 1st when we sort of set the next low.

But in all of these cases, in all these cases, the next large drop tend to bottom out in sort of that April, May, April to June timeframe, if you want to be really flexible, April to June timeframe, is when you then got like the next lower low in the bear market and every other move ended up just being a lower high, okay?

strategy_components.description: But ultimately, it's hard to assume, it's hard to ignore this trend, right?
Remember, Bitcoin sells off first and it eventually works its way down the risk curve and gets to sort of higher risk assets.
And Bitcoin is, you know, risky.
It's further up the risk curve than the S&P 500, like an index fund that tracks the US markets.
